nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, heavily vegetated blackwater streams, drainage ditches, and small tributaries; often found among leaf litter and dense riparian vegetation in coastal lowland drainages

decorPreferences: caves or coconut shells for spawning sites
dense planting (Java fern, Amazon swords, Vallisneria)
driftwood and leaf litter for natural blackwater feel
open mid-water swimming space in front of planted/caved areas

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of foods: high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, and bloodworms. Live and frozen invertebrates are especially valued by females during conditioning for breeding. Will also pick at algae and biofilm. Feed small amounts 1–2 times daily; avoid overfeeding in soft-water setups where water quality can decline quickly.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
dwarf gouramis (with caution in smaller tanks)
avoidWith: similar-looking Apistogramma spp. (aggression and hybridisation risk)
large or boisterous tankmates that outcompete for food
shrimp in breeding tanks (fry protection aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Nannacara anomala (male); standard reference photograph.
Wikimedia Commons image showing male coloration; check current license status before use.

summary: A beginner-friendly South American dwarf cichlid from Guyana and Suriname. Males display vivid gold and green iridescence; breeding females develop a dramatic black-and-white checkerboard pattern. Cave spawners that form monogamous pairs — more adaptable to varying water conditions than most Apistogramma.
careNotes: The Golden Dwarf Cichlid is one of the most forgiving dwarf cichlids for aquarists stepping into the group for the first time. Unlike many Apistogramma species, N. anomala tolerates a relatively broad pH range (6.0–7.5) and moderate hardness, making it accessible in areas with slightly harder tap water. A minimum 60 L tank with a 60 cm footprint suits a bonded pair, though a larger tank (80–100 L) is recommended if kept with dither fish. Provide multiple cave options (coconut halves, clay pots, or commercial cave ornaments) so the female can claim a preferred spawning site and the male can be excluded once eggs are laid without having nowhere to retreat. Dense planting along the back and sides, combined with driftwood and dried leaf litter (Indian almond or oak leaves), replicates the blackwater biotope and helps mellow aggression. Males reach 6–8 cm and are far more colourful, displaying iridescent gold, green, and blue scales; females stay smaller (4–5 cm) and normally show a subtle brown pattern that transforms dramatically into bold black-and-white checkerboard markings when guarding eggs or fry. N. anomala is often confused with the closely related N. taenia (Crossbanded Dwarf Cichlid); N. taenia males have a more distinct horizontal band and are slightly more demanding in terms of water quality. Males can be territorial towards other males and will defend their area of the tank, but conspecific aggression is generally limited to chasing rather than sustained injury in adequately decorated setups. A 10–20% weekly water change is sufficient to maintain water quality. Avoid keeping with shrimp in breeding tanks as the female becomes highly aggressive when guarding fry.
breedingNotes: N. anomala is a cave-spawning dwarf cichlid that forms monogamous pair bonds rather than the haremic arrangements typical of many Apistogramma species. Condition both sexes with live or frozen foods before introducing them to a well-planted breeding tank. The female selects and cleans a cave interior and lays 80–200 adhesive eggs, which she guards fiercely. The male is usually driven away from the immediate spawning site but continues to patrol the outer territory; in small tanks he may need to be removed once spawning begins to prevent female stress or injury. Eggs hatch in 2–3 days at 25–26°C and the female moves the wriggling larvae to pre-dug pits in the substrate. Fry become free-swimming after a further 4–5 days and can be fed infusoria, vinegar eels, or commercial fry foods initially, progressing to baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii) within the first week. The female provides exemplary parental care and will shepherd the fry for 3–4 weeks. Breedability is rated easy: the pair will often spawn with no special intervention once settled and conditioned.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nannacara anomala
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 8 cm, females to 4.5 cm), temperature 20–28°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 1–15°H, native range (Guyana, Suriname), habitat, diet, breeding behaviour, and conspecific notes. Confirmed cave-spawning monogamous pair-bonding strategy and female checkerboard coloration during breeding.

additional.name: FishBase — Nannacara anomala

additional.name: Cichlid Room Companion — Nannacara anomala
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid reference; confirmed native range in coastal Guyana and Suriname lowland streams, blackwater biotope, breeding behaviour details including egg counts (80–200), fry care duration, and male territorial behaviour. Also clarified distinction from N. taenia.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner forming a monogamous pair bond. Female lays 80–200 eggs in a cleaned cave interior and provides sole parental care; male guards the outer territory but is often expelled from the cave site. Eggs hatch in 2–3 days; fry free-swimming by day 5–7. Female shepherds fry for 3–4 weeks. Feed fry infusoria then baby brine shrimp. Male may need removal in small tanks to prevent female stress.
